specification.topic-statement: The rendering context manages the terminal output buffer, converts logical text and cursor positions into ANSI escape sequences, and serializes the resulting framebuffer to the terminal (or, in headless mode, to a JSON representation for remote inspection).

scope.in-scope: Output buffer management and growth strategy, newline-to-CRLF conversion, cursor position calculation from a byte offset (accounting for UTF-8 width, ANSI sequences, and line wrapping), atomic rendering to the terminal, and headless framebuffer serialization to structured JSON.
scope.boundary: How the seven display layers are stacked and clipped before being written into the output buffer is covered by the layer rendering specification.
How the terminal is switched to raw mode and the alternate screen is managed is handled by the terminal subsystem and is out of scope here.

field.name: terminal width
field.note: terminal width in columns; fixed at context creation

field.name: terminal height
field.note: terminal height in rows; fixed at context creation

field.name: terminal file descriptor
field.note: the TTY to write to; -1 in headless mode

field.name: output buffer
field.note: accumulates ANSI escape sequences and text before the atomic write; never shrinks

field.name: buffer used
field.note: bytes currently occupied in the output buffer

field.name: buffer capacity
field.note: total allocated bytes in the output buffer; grows by 1.5× or to required size, whichever is larger

behavior.output-buffer-management:
All rendered content is accumulated in a dynamically growing byte array before being written to the terminal. When an append would exceed the current capacity, the buffer is reallocated to the larger of 1.5× the current capacity or the exact size required. The buffer never shrinks during a session.
Rendering is atomic: a single render pass assembles all content into the buffer, then writes it to the terminal in one system call. This prevents visual flicker and partial-frame display.
behavior.newline-conversion: When text is appended to the output buffer, embedded newline characters (`\n`) are converted to carriage-return-plus-newline pairs (`\r\n`). This conversion is necessary for raw-mode terminals where the kernel no longer performs CR/LF translation. The required buffer space is pre-calculated by counting newlines before the append.
behavior.cursor-position-calculation:
Given a byte offset into a text string, the rendering context calculates the terminal row and column where the cursor should be positioned. The calculation iterates from the start of the text, maintaining a running column count and row count:
- Newline characters reset the column to zero and increment the row. - ANSI CSI escape sequences (detected via the ANSI parser) are skipped without advancing the column. - Each UTF-8 codepoint is decoded and its display width (0, 1, or 2 columns) is added to the column count. - When the column count reaches the terminal width, it wraps to zero and the row increments (to account for soft line-wrapping).
The resulting row and column are converted to a 1-based ANSI cursor positioning sequence (`\x1b[row;colH`) and written to the output buffer.
behavior.atomic-render-sequence:
A complete render to the terminal follows this sequence:
1. The cursor is hidden (`\x1b[?25l`). 2. The cursor is moved to the home position (`\x1b[H`). 3. All layer content (already assembled by the compositor) is written to the output buffer. 4. The cursor is repositioned to the calculated input cursor location. 5. The cursor is made visible (`\x1b[?25h`). 6. The entire output buffer is written to the terminal file descriptor in a single call. 7. The buffer used counter is reset to zero for the next frame.
behavior.headless-framebuffer-serialization:
In headless mode (no TTY attached), the output buffer is serialized to structured JSON instead of being written to a file descriptor. The serializer:
1. Parses the ANSI escape sequences in the buffer to extract per-character styling attributes (foreground color, bold, dim, reverse video). 2. Groups consecutive characters that share the same styling into spans. 3. Reconstructs line structure by detecting carriage-return-plus-newline sequences. 4. Extracts the cursor position from the ANSI cursor positioning sequence. 5. Emits a JSON object containing an array of rows (each row being an array of styled spans) and the cursor position.
This JSON representation is what the control socket client reads when a remote process calls the read-framebuffer operation.
behavior.last-row-handling: When the last rendered row of content exactly fills the terminal's last physical row, writing a trailing CRLF would cause the terminal to scroll. This is detected and suppressed: the CRLF that would follow the final row is omitted, preventing unintended scrolling of the terminal display.
